subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 16! In this unit you will become skilled at collecting, sorting, displaying and reading data. The word *data* simply means information – often numbers or facts that we gather to answer a question. Every day, people around you turn data into pictures so that it becomes easy to understand at a glance. When Amina counts how many goats, camels and cattle her family keeps and draws it as a chart, when Yusuf records how many cars pass the masjid before each prayer, or when Halima keeps a tally of which fruits sell best at the Hargeisa market, they are all working with data.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Create and interpret Carroll diagrams to sort and classify data by yes/no properties.
Create and interpret Venn diagrams with two and three sets, including the overlaps.
Represent data using pictograms with a clear key, including partial symbols.
Create bar charts with a sensible scale, labelled axes and a title.
Read and interpret information from many kinds of data display.
Compare two data sets using grouped or side-by-side bar charts.
Choose the most appropriate display for a given set of data.
Make observations, spot patterns, and draw sensible conclusions from data.

concepts.title: Sorting with Carroll Diagrams
concepts.explanation: Before we can display data, we often need to sort it – to put each item into the right group. One neat tool for sorting is the Carroll diagram, named after the writer Lewis Carroll, who loved logic puzzles. A Carroll diagram sorts items using only yes/no answers to two properties. Because each property is either true or not true, the diagram has exactly four boxes, showing every possible combination. Suppose we sort shapes by two properties: *Is it red?* and *Is it a triangle?* The grid looks like this, with the…
concepts.example: Read any box by combining its row and its column. The top-left box holds shapes that are red and a triangle. The top-right box holds shapes that are not red but still a triangle – for example a blue triangle. The…

concepts.title: Sorting with Venn Diagrams
concepts.explanation: A Venn diagram is another way to sort, but it shows relationships using overlapping circles. Each circle stands for one property. An item that has that property goes inside the circle; an item that does not stays outside it. Where two circles overlap – a region called the intersection – we place items that have both properties at once. Anything with neither property sits outside all the circles but inside the surrounding rectangle. Imagine two circles labelled *Multiples of 2* and *Multiples of 3*, and we sort the…
concepts.example: So the finished diagram looks like this: Multiples of 2 only – 2, 4, 8, 10; Both (the overlap) – 6, 12; Multiples of 3 only – 3, 9; Neither – 1, 5, 7, 11. A common wrong idea is to write 6 twice, once in each circle. Do…

concepts.title: Pictograms and Their Keys
concepts.explanation: A pictogram (sometimes called a pictograph) displays data using pictures or symbols. Each symbol stands for a fixed number of items, and that number is written in the key. The key is the most important part of any pictogram – without it, you cannot tell what the pictures mean. A row of five apples could stand for five apples, or fifty, or five hundred, depending on the key. Here is a pictogram showing how many books six pupils at Nuur Academy read in one month. Read the key first: each diamond stands for 2 books.
concepts.example: To read any row, count the symbols and multiply by the key value. Amina has 5 diamonds, and 5 × 2 = 10 books. Halima has 6 diamonds, and 6 × 2 = 12 books, the most of anyone. Ibrahim has just 2 diamonds, so 2 × 2 = 4…

concepts.title: Bar Charts – Building and Reading
concepts.explanation: A bar chart (or bar graph) shows data using rectangular bars. The height of each bar shows the value for that category: the taller the bar, the larger the amount. Bar charts are the workhorse of data display because they make it so easy to compare many categories at once and to spot the biggest and smallest at a glance. Every good bar chart has the same parts. The vertical axis (up the side) carries the scale – the numbers, which must start at zero. The horizontal axis (along the bottom) carries the categories.…
concepts.example: Here is a bar chart showing the favourite colours of a class. Picture bars rising from a baseline of zero:

concepts.title: Comparing Data Sets
concepts.explanation: Often we want to compare two sets of data – boys and girls, this week and last week, Class A and Class B. Bar charts are perfect for this, and there are two neat ways to do it. In a side-by-side display, you draw two separate bar charts using the same scale and categories, then place them next to each other so the eye can compare matching bars. In a grouped bar chart, you draw two bars in each category, sitting right beside each other, using different colours or patterns and a key to tell them apart. Suppose we…
concepts.example: A grouped bar chart of this data puts a boys' bar and a girls' bar side by side in each colour group. Straight away you can read the story. Blue is the boys' favourite (12), while purple is by far the girls' favourite…

concepts.title: Interpreting and Drawing Conclusions
concepts.explanation: Building a display is only half the work; the real prize is interpreting it – explaining what it tells you. When you look at any chart or diagram, train yourself to ask a set of questions. Which category has the highest value, and which the lowest? What is the difference between two categories? Is there a pattern or trend – do the values rise, fall, or stay level as you move along? Are there any unusual values that stand out from the rest? And what is the total across all the categories? Once you have made your…
concepts.example: Building a display is only half the work; the real prize is interpreting it – explaining what it tells you. When you look at any chart or diagram, train yourself to ask a set of questions. Which category has the highest…

methods.title: How to read a pictogram
methods.example: Sort the numbers 1 to 10 by *even?* and *greater than 5?*
steps: Read the key to find what one symbol is worth.
Count the symbols in the row (including any part symbols).
Multiply whole symbols by the key value.
Add on the value of any half or quarter symbol.

methods.title: How to draw a bar chart
methods.example: Sort the numbers 1 to 15 into *Multiples of 2* and *Multiples of 5*. Where does 10 go?
steps: Choose a scale that fits your largest value and starts at zero.
Draw and label the two axes; add a title.
Draw each bar to the right height, all the same width.
Leave equal gaps between the bars.

methods.title: How to sort with a Venn diagram
methods.example: In a pictogram, each ☺ = 5 vehicles. Cars show 6 symbols. How many cars?
steps: Label one circle for each property.
Test each item against every property.
Put items with both properties in the overlap.
Put items with no property outside the circles, inside the box.

methods.title: Partial symbols
methods.example: Each symbol represents 4 ice creams. How do you show 18 ice creams?
steps: Solution: 18 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 2.
That is four whole symbols (4 × 4 = 16) plus a half symbol for the remaining 2 (because half of 4 is 2).
So draw four full symbols and one half symbol: 16 + 2 = 18.

methods.title: Read a bar chart
methods.example: A bar chart of museum visitors shows Mon 150, Tue 200, Wed 250, Thu 200, Fri 250, Sat 300, Sun 150. What was the total for the week, and how many more came on Saturday than Monday?
steps: Solution: Add every bar for the total: 150 + 200 + 250 + 200 + 250 + 300 + 150 = 1550 visitors.
Saturday had 300 and Monday had 150, so the difference is 300 − 150 = 150 more visitors on Saturday.
Check the read a bar chart result carefully.

methods.title: Choose a scale
methods.example: You must draw a bar chart of team points: 45, 60, 35, 50, 55. What scale should the side axis use?
steps: Solution: The largest value is 60, so a scale going up in 1s would be far too tall.
Counting up in 10s (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60) is sensible – it fits 60 comfortably and every value can still be read.
Start the scale at zero and keep the steps equal.

workedExamples.title: Place items in a Carroll diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Sort the numbers 1 to 10 by *even?* and *greater than 5?*
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ask both questions of each number. Even and greater than 5: 6, 8, 10. Even and not greater than 5: 2, 4. Odd and greater than 5: 7, 9. Odd and not greater than 5: 1, 3, 5. Every number lands in exactly one box, and together the four boxes hold all ten numbers.

workedExamples.title: Read a two-circle Venn diagram
workedExamples.prompt: Sort the numbers 1 to 15 into *Multiples of 2* and *Multiples of 5*. Where does 10 go?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiples of 2 are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14. Multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15. The number 10 appears in both lists, so it belongs in the overlap. Multiples of 2 only: 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 14. Multiples of 5 only: 5, 15. Neither: 1, 3, 7, 9, 11, 13.

workedExamples.title: Read a pictogram
workedExamples.prompt: In a pictogram, each ☺ = 5 vehicles. Cars show 6 symbols. How many cars?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply the number of symbols by the key value: 6 × 5 = 30 cars. Reading the key first is essential – if you forgot it and thought each symbol meant 1, you would answer 6, which is wrong by a lot.

workedExamples.title: Partial symbols
workedExamples.prompt: Each symbol represents 4 ice creams. How do you show 18 ice creams?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 18 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 2. That is four whole symbols (4 × 4 = 16) plus a half symbol for the remaining 2 (because half of 4 is 2). So draw four full symbols and one half symbol: 16 + 2 = 18.

workedExamples.title: Read a bar chart
workedExamples.prompt: A bar chart of museum visitors shows Mon 150, Tue 200, Wed 250, Thu 200, Fri 250, Sat 300, Sun 150. What was the total for the week, and how many more came on Saturday than Monday?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add every bar for the total: 150 + 200 + 250 + 200 + 250 + 300 + 150 = 1550 visitors. Saturday had 300 and Monday had 150, so the difference is 300 − 150 = 150 more visitors on Saturday.

workedExamples.title: Choose a scale
workedExamples.prompt: You must draw a bar chart of team points: 45, 60, 35, 50, 55. What scale should the side axis use?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The largest value is 60, so a scale going up in 1s would be far too tall. Counting up in 10s (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60) is sensible – it fits 60 comfortably and every value can still be read. Start the scale at zero and keep the steps equal.

workedExamples.title: Compare two data sets
workedExamples.prompt: In a grouped bar chart, purple was chosen by 2 boys and 14 girls. Describe the difference.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the smaller from the larger: 14 − 2 = 12. So 12 more girls than boys chose purple. This is the biggest difference of any colour, which suggests purple is much more popular with girls than with boys in this class.

workedExamples.title: Interpret and conclude
workedExamples.prompt: A bar chart of theme-park visitors peaks in July and August and is lowest from November to February. Write one observation and one conclusion.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: An observation states a fact from the chart: ‘The park had the most visitors in July and August.’ A conclusion explains why, linked to the world: ‘The park is busiest in summer, probably because the weather is warm and schools are closed, so families have more time to visit.’ Notice the observation reports the data while the conclusion gives a sensible reason.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Sort the numbers 1 to 12 into a Carroll diagram of *even?* and *multiple of 3?* List all four boxes.
workedExamples.solution: Even & mult of 3: 6, 12. Even & not: 2, 4, 8, 10. Odd & mult of 3: 3, 9. Odd & not: 1, 5, 7, 11.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A pictogram of vehicles uses ☺ = 5. Cars = 6 symbols, buses = 2, bicycles = 3. How many of each, and how many altogether?

workedExamples.title: Choose the best display for each
workedExamples.prompt: Choose the best display for each: a) comparing points of five teams; b) numbers that are multiples of both 2 and 5.
workedExamples.solution: a) bar chart; b) Venn diagram.

workedExamples.solution: 1) 30 sits in the centre where all three circles meet (multiple of 2, 3 and

activities.title: Household Sorting with a Carroll Diagram
activities.materials: a collection of household objects (spoons, cups, shoes) and paper.
steps: Choose two yes/no properties, for example ‘Is it made of metal?’ and ‘Is it round?’
Draw a Carroll diagram with four boxes and label the rows and columns.
Sort each object into the correct box by asking both questions.
Count how many objects landed in each box. Which box has the most?
Try again with different properties, such as colour and size, and see how the sorting changes.

activities.title: Family Venn Diagram
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and the numbers 1 to 20 written on small cards.
steps: Draw two large overlapping circles and label them ‘Multiples of 2’ and ‘Multiples of 3’.
Take each number card and decide where it belongs – left, right, overlap, or outside.
Place the ‘both’ numbers (like 6, 12, 18) carefully in the overlap.
Ask a family member to check your diagram and explain any tricky placements.

activities.title: Traffic Pictogram Investigation
activities.materials: a safe spot to watch a road, paper and a pencil.
steps: For ten minutes, tally each vehicle you see: cars, bajaj, buses, motorbikes.
Decide on a key – for example, one symbol for every 2 vehicles.
Draw a pictogram of your results, using partial symbols where needed.
Write two things your pictogram shows, such as which vehicle was most common.

activities.title: Market Bar Chart
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler, coloured pencils.
steps: Ask five family members their favourite fruit (mango, banana, papaya, dates, orange).
Make a tally chart, then count the total for each fruit.
Draw a bar chart with a scale starting at zero, equal-width bars, labels and a title.
Which fruit is most popular? Which is least? Write down the total number of people you asked.

activities.title: The Guess-and-Check Data Game
activities.materials: a bar chart or pictogram you have drawn, and a partner.
steps: Show your partner the display for a few seconds, then cover it.
Ask them to guess the tallest bar, the smallest, and the total.
Uncover the display and check their guesses together.
Swap roles and play again with a different display.

activities.title: Compare Two Weeks (Family Investigation)
activities.materials: paper and pencil, over two weeks.
steps: Choose something to count each day, such as how many cups of tea your family drinks.
Record the daily totals for Week 1, then for Week 2.
Draw a grouped bar chart with Week 1 and Week 2 bars side by side for each day, using a key.
Write two observations comparing the two weeks – which was higher, and where the biggest difference was.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which display was easiest to read, and why? What was the trickiest part of collecting or sorting your data? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Pictogram Rule
rules.text: Read the key first. Value = number of symbols × key value. A half symbol is worth half of the key value.

rules.title: Bar Chart Rule
rules.text: The scale must start at zero, with equal steps all the way up. Bars are the same width, evenly spaced, with labelled axes and a title.

rules.title: Venn Diagram Rule
rules.text: One circle = one property. The overlap is for items with BOTH properties. Circle size means nothing; count the items written inside.

terms: Information collected to answer a question
A grid sorting items by two yes/no properties
Overlapping circles showing how groups relate
The overlap for items with both properties
A display using symbols, each worth a set amount
What each symbol or colour stands for
The numbers up the side (axis) of a chart
How many times something is counted
The value that appears most often
commonMistakes: Multiply symbols by the key value
Place it in the overlap only
Always start the scale at zero
Judging a Venn circle by size

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, Sagal tallies drinks sold: camel milk IIII IIII (9), tea IIII I (6), juice IIII (5). Draw this as a pictogram (symbol = 1) and say which sold most and the total sold.
realProblems.answer: 1) Camel milk 9, tea 6, juice 5 – camel milk sold most; total 20 drinks.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Camel milk 9, tea 6, juice 5 – camel milk sold most; total 20 drinks.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf counts vehicles before Asr with each symbol worth 5: cars 8 symbols, bajaj 5 symbols, buses 2 symbols. How many of each, and how many more cars than buses?
realProblems.answer: Cars 40, bajaj 25, buses 10; 40 − 10 = 30 more cars than buses.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Cars 40, bajaj 25, buses 10; 40 − 10 = 30 more cars than buses.

realProblems.prompt: A grouped bar chart compares favourite colours for boys and girls: purple 2 boys and 14 girls; blue 12 boys and 7 girls. Which colour shows the biggest difference, and how big is it?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A school of 100 pupils travels like this: 45 walk, 30 by car, 15 cycle, 10 by bus. What fraction walk, and which display would show this data well?
realProblems.answer: 45 out of 100 = 45/100 = 9/20 walk; a bar chart (or pictogram) shows it well.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 45 out of 100 = 45/100 = 9/20 walk; a bar chart (or pictogram) shows it well.

realProblems.prompt: A theme-park bar chart peaks in July (45 thousand) and is lowest in December (5 thousand). How many more visitors came in July than December, and give two reasons for the summer peak.
realProblems.answer: 45 − 5 = 40 thousand more in July; reasons: warm weather and school holidays in summer.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 45 − 5 = 40 thousand more in July; reasons: warm weather and school holidays in summer.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A pictogram with symbols worth 5 shows Class A collected 15 symbols of recycling and Class B collected 12. How much more did Class A collect?
Explain why the size of a circle in a Venn diagram does not tell you how many items are inside it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Total 1550; busiest Saturday (300); average 1550 ÷ 7 ≈ 221 per day.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Comparing Data Sets.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Often we want to compare two sets of data – boys and girls, this week and last week, Class A and Class B. Bar charts are perfect for this, and there are two neat ways to do it. In a side-by-side display, you draw two separate bar charts using the same scale and categories, then place them next to each other so the eye can compare matching bars. In a grouped bar chart, you draw two bars in each category, sitting right beside each other, using different colours or patterns and a key to tell them apart. Suppose we…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Interpreting and Drawing Conclusions.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Building a display is only half the work; the real prize is interpreting it – explaining what it tells you. When you look at any chart or diagram, train yourself to ask a set of questions. Which category has the highest value, and which the lowest? What is the difference between two categories? Is there a pattern or trend – do the values rise, fall, or stay level as you move along? Are there any unusual values that stand out from the rest? And what is the total across all the categories? Once you have made your…

selfAssessment: I can create and interpret Carroll diagrams to sort and classify data by yes/no properties.
I can create and interpret Venn diagrams with two and three sets, including the overlaps.
I can represent data using pictograms with a clear key, including partial symbols.
I can create bar charts with a sensible scale, labelled axes and a title.
I can read and interpret information from many kinds of data display.
I can compare two data sets using grouped or side-by-side bar charts.
I can choose the most appropriate display for a given set of data.
I can make observations, spot patterns, and draw sensible conclusions from data.
